title: State makes workplace safety legally binding
full_text:
Employers in NSW will be subject to expanded work, health and safety obligations after the state’s WHS codes of practice became legally binding this week.
The so-called “comply or justify” laws means the codes of practice that provide businesses with practical guidance on how to meet their WHS legal obligations are now legally enforceable minimum standards that workplaces must comply with or risk breaching the Work Health and Safety Act.
Codes of Practice are generally specialised to a particular industry such as tunnelling or a particular workplace hazard such as managing the risks of respirable crystalline silica in the workplace.
Under the change, it will be mandatory for a person conducting a business or undertaking to either comply with the applicable code or manage the risk to an equivalent or higher standard.
The NSW government said the change meant the state had the strongest position on codes of practice in the country, demonstrating the progress made in aligning the workers compensation, industrial relations, and workplace health and safety systems to achieve better outcomes for workers and employers.
NSW Minister for Work Health and Safety Sophie Cotsis said the reforms were about preventing injuries before they happened by giving businesses clear expectations about “what good workplace safety looks like”.
“For the first time, approved codes of practice aren’t simply guidance, they are now legally enforceable minimum standards that workplaces must comply with,” she said.
In anticipation of the codes becoming mandatory, SafeWork NSW has spent the past year updating the codes with representatives of business, unions and independent experts.
SafeWork NSW said its four priority areas for this financial year were falls from heights; managing psychosocial risks; exposure to hazardous substances; and using mobile plant, vehicles and fixed machinery.
Over the past 12 months, more than 600 incidents, including five fatalities, related to falls from heights were reported to SafeWork NSW, while over 500 incidents and eight fatalities involved mobile plant, vehicles or fixed machinery.
SafeWork NSW received more than 2200 requests for service and was notified of more than 190 workplace incidents involving psychosocial hazards.
Inspectors issued more than 145 silica-related notices, with over 45 cases of silicosis reported.
Meanwhile, employers have praised the Albanese government for new legislation that offers relief to employers impacted by millions of dollars in debts associated with the coalmining long-service leave scheme, The government will waive 20 per cent of outstanding amounts and give companies six years to backpay leave entitlements in instalments while ensuring workers receive full entitlements, some dating back 15 years. Employers are required to pay a levy of 2.7 per cent on the eligible wages of employees covered by the scheme.
“We’ve worked with industry and unions to deliver a practical, fair solution that supports workers and gives employers a clear path to do the right thing,” Workplace Relations Minister Amanda Rishworth said.
The Minerals Council of Australia thanked the government for the legislation and congratulated Labor for heeding the concerns of affected businesses. Australian Industry Group CEO Innes Willox said the legislation achieved a balance between the interests of all parties.
